skill.name: frontend-mobile-security-xss-scan
skill.description: Guides frontend XSS review for React, Vue, Angular, and JavaScript code by checking unsafe rendering, DOM manipulation, URL handling, and sanitization gaps.
skill.summary: Review frontend code for XSS risks and safer rendering patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows they are Markdown examples for detecting XSS rather than active malicious code. No prompt injection, credential access, hidden network exfiltration, or confirmed malware intent was found. Residual risk is low because the skill suggests filesystem scanning and optional command-line tools that should run only with user consent.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Reading Example Is Expected Scanner Behavior
low_findings.description: The fs.readFile usage is inside a TypeScript example that scans source files for XSS patterns. This is expected for a local security scanner, but users should limit scans to intended project paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is clearly inside a documented XSSScanner example and reads files selected by the scanner. No evidence found of secret collection or transmission.

low_findings.title: Command-Line Tool Examples Need User Consent
low_findings.description: The skill suggests npm, eslint, semgrep, and node commands for optional scanning workflows. These are normal security-review tools, but they can install packages or inspect local files if executed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands appear in a fenced bash example and match the stated purpose of XSS scanning. They are not hidden execution, but they still require explicit user approval before use.

low_findings.title: Sanitized innerHTML Example Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: The innerHTML assignment uses a DOMPurify.sanitize result in a secure coding example. This is instructional content and does not create an active marketplace execution risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sanitizer call directly precedes the innerHTML assignment in a section labeled secure. The surrounding text recommends sanitization rather than unsafe rendering.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Unsupported
low_findings.description: Static high-risk alerts for weak cryptography match security-related text and XSS examples, not cryptographic code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, encryption misuse, or credential handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the reported locations found XSS scanner fields, string matching, and URL protocol validation. None of the cited lines contain cryptographic algorithms.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Documentation Matches
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance alerts point to XSS checklist text and a Semgrep XSS command. No evidence found of host discovery, port scanning, or network probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss XSS prevention and optional Semgrep configuration. They do not describe discovering systems, services, or network targets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Reading In Scanner Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample scanner reads JavaScript files to inspect source content. This is legitimate for XSS review but should stay scoped to user-approved repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem access is explicit and tied to the scanner input path. No evidence found that the content is sent outside the workspace.

dangerous_patterns.title: Optional External Security Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The bash example includes package installation and scanner commands. These commands are common in security review but should not be run without user confirmation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible documentation examples, not automatic execution hooks. Risk depends on whether an assistant executes them in a user environment.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTML Sink Appears Only In Sanitized Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The innerHTML sink is shown after sanitization as a secure pattern. Assistants should preserve the sanitizer requirement when adapting this example.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example imports DOMPurify, sanitizes user input, and then assigns the cleaned value. This supports a false-positive classification for the raw innerHTML alert.

content.value_statement: Frontend XSS flaws often hide in rendering, URL handling, and framework escape hatches. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through focused client-side security review.
actual_capabilities: Reviews JavaScript and framework code for unsafe HTML rendering patterns.
Checks React, Vue, and Angular escape hatches that can bypass safe rendering.
Highlights risky URL assignment and protocol handling patterns.
Recommends safer alternatives such as textContent, DOMPurify, and URL validation.
Organizes XSS findings by severity, location, impact, and suggested fix.
limitations: It is guidance for review and does not guarantee complete vulnerability coverage.
It may need repository access to inspect real source files.
It can produce false positives when sanitization is handled elsewhere.
It does not replace dynamic testing, browser testing, or professional penetration testing.

use_cases.title: Review A Pull Request For XSS
use_cases.description: Check changed frontend files for unsafe rendering, URL handling, and missing sanitization before merge.

use_cases.title: Prepare A Security Audit Checklist
use_cases.description: Convert frontend XSS concerns into a structured review checklist for manual or assisted code review.

use_cases.title: Harden Framework Components
use_cases.description: Find risky React, Vue, or Angular patterns and replace them with safer framework-aware alternatives.

prompt_templates.title: Basic XSS Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small component or file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this frontend component for XSS risks. Focus on unsafe HTML rendering, user input, and simple fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Framework Escape Hatch Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate review for React, Vue, or Angular code.
prompt_templates.prompt: Scan this frontend code for framework-specific XSS risks. Check dangerouslySetInnerHTML, v-html, sanitizer bypasses, and unsafe template patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Repository XSS Triage
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review across multiple source files.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this repository for frontend XSS issues. Group findings by severity, file, affected pattern, exploitability, and recommended remediation.

prompt_templates.title: Remediation Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning after findings are identified.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a prioritized remediation plan for these XSS findings. Include secure alternatives, validation steps, and remaining risk after each fix.

output_examples.input: Review a React component that renders HTML from a CMS field.
output_examples.output: The report flags unsafe raw HTML rendering, explains the XSS impact, and recommends sanitizing trusted HTML before render.

output_examples.input: Audit Vue templates that use v-html in several pages.
output_examples.output: The review separates high-risk user-controlled HTML from lower-risk static content and suggests v-text or sanitizer-backed rendering.

output_examples.input: Check URL assignments built from query parameters.
output_examples.output: The analysis identifies protocol injection risk and recommends URL parsing, allowlisted protocols, and safe fallback behavior.

best_practices: Review real data flow before marking a sink exploitable.
Prefer framework-safe rendering APIs before adding sanitizer exceptions.
Validate fixes with tests that include encoded and malformed payloads.
anti_patterns: Do not treat every innerHTML occurrence as exploitable without context.
Do not bypass framework sanitization unless the trust boundary is documented.
Do not run installation or scanner commands without explicit user approval.

faq.question: What languages and frameworks does this skill target?
faq.answer: It targets JavaScript frontend code, including React, Vue, Angular, and vanilla DOM usage.

faq.question: Does it automatically exploit XSS vulnerabilities?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on detection, risk explanation, and remediation guidance.

faq.question: Can it review an entire repository?
faq.answer: Yes, when the assistant has access to the repository files and the user approves the review scope.

faq.question: Does it require running external tools?
faq.answer: No. External tools are optional examples and should only run with explicit user consent.

faq.question: Can it reduce false positives?
faq.answer: Yes. It asks the assistant to consider data flow, sanitizer use, and framework context.

faq.question: Is this a replacement for penetration testing?
faq.answer: No. It supports code review and remediation, but dynamic testing may still be required.
